title: Hello!
text:
Hi there! Welcome to my teaching and training website. This is a repository for all the teaching and training materials that I am currently using or have previously delivered.
My background is in Biomedical Engineering, and my research interests include:

Cardiovascular Mechanobiology and Tissue Engineering
Data Science Education in Low-Resource Settings
Accessible Data Visualization

I am also a Certified Carpentries Instructor and a trainer with the Digital Research Academy. In these roles my goal is to train learners on the basics of computational thinking. Additionally, I am an ardent promoter of using open-source tools (such as R/Python) to solve data analysis and visualization problems.

title: Properties of Biomaterials (EBM 301)
text:
Prerequisites: EBM 200
Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Laboratory Sessions: TBD
Instructor: William Okech, PhD
Email Me
Course Website: https://wokech.github.io/courses.html –> select your course/year


Biomaterials play an important role in the life sciences and medicine. They are used to aid in the restoration of function and promote healing after injury and disease. Biomaterials can be of natural, synthetic, or hybrid origin, and they are designed to interact with living systems in a safe and compatible manner. In this course, we will look at various materials including metals, ceramics, plastics, and glass, as well as living cells and tissues that are used to develop biomaterials. Additionally, the course will explore material characterization and testing methods, biocompatibility, and important considerations for the design and manufacture of materials for biomedical applications. In addition to lectures (where relevant), laboratory activities and demonstrations will be performed. Lastly, case studies and contemporary examples will be used to explore new technologies.




Atomic Theory of Materials and Bond Theory of Solids
Phase Diagrams and Phase Transformations
Mechanics of Materials
Major Biomaterial Groups (Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, and Composites)
Surface vs Bulk Properties of Materials
Material Degradation
Biomaterial-Host Tissue Interactions
Drug Delivery Systems and Tissue Engineering





Lectures: 2 hours per week
Tutorials: 2 hours per week
Laboratory Exercises: At least four experiments per semester, with each lab/practical session 3 hours long.





Regular Examination at end of Semester: 70%
Continuous Assessment: 30% where 10% shall be continuous assessment tests, 5% shall be assignments, and 15% shall be labs.
The students must complete and pass all the labs before they can be allowed to proceed to the next class.

title: About
text: This is the teaching website for William Okech

title: Biomechanics I (EBM 403)
text:
Prerequisites: Mechanics, physiology, anatomy
Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Instructor: William Okech, PhD
Email Me
Course Website: https://wokech.github.io/courses.html –> select your course/year


Biomechanics is the application of the principles of mechanics (both solid and fluid) to biological systems and living organisms. These biological systems range from whole organisms to cells and tissues, as well as proteins. Various subfields of biomechanics exist including but not limited to cardiovascular, forensic, sports, comparative, and computational. In this course, we will introduce the basic concepts of biomechanics with a focus on the musculoskeletal system. Moreover, the learners will explore the mechanics of cells and tissues in the musculoskeletal system, the design of principles of orthopedic implants, and injury biomechanics. In addition to lectures (where relevant), laboratory activities and demonstrations will be performed. Lastly, case studies and contemporary examples will be used to explore new technologies.




Biomechanics of Joints
Ergonomics
Work Physiology and Industrial Safety
Basics of Fluid Mechanics
Prosthetics and Orthotics





Lectures: 2 hours per week
Tutorials: 2 hours per week





Regular Examination at end of Semester: 70%
Continuous Assessment: 30% where 10% shall be continuous assessment tests, 5% shall be assignments, and 15% shall be labs.
The students must complete and pass all the labs before they can be allowed to proceed to the next class.
